summary: Patrick McKenzie, Keith Perhac, and Amy Hoy discuss the trajectory from consulting to product businesses, pricing strategies for SaaS companies selling to businesses, and the lifestyle benefits of running your own software company. Amy describes building Freckle and her 30x500 course after leaving consulting, emphasizing that developers and designers absolutely do buy software despite conventional wisdom. The conversation covers why SaaS pricing grids should include high-tier enterprise plans, why selling to businesses beats B2C, and why competitors validate rather than threaten a market. They conclude with a philosophical discussion about work-life balance, arguing that the culture of overwork is self-imposed and that product businesses can generate substantial revenue on remarkably few hours of actual work.
key_points: Businesses are not price-sensitive the way individuals are — purchases under $500 often require zero signatures, making SaaS pricing grids that top out at $20-30 a missed opportunity
The conventional wisdom that 'developers don't buy software' is demonstrably false, as evidenced by GitHub, Basecamp, FreshBooks, Harvest, and dozens of other successful developer-focused products
Competitors are validation, not threats — a saturated market means proven demand, and a small company only needs to serve the segment being poorly served by incumbents
Selling to businesses lets you price on value (time saved, revenue generated) rather than competing on cost, which is fundamentally more sustainable

The culture of overwork in startups and corporate jobs is largely self-imposed cargo-culting — most people have only 2-4 genuinely productive hours per day, and successful product businesses can thrive on minimal weekly hours
money_quotes: If you routinely hang around people who run businesses, your perception of the world is going to get influenced by people who run businesses, and if you're routinely hanging around people who will perpetually have a business some day you have a risk of that warping your perspective on things.

Anybody who's doing a SaaS pricing grid where the top price tops out at $20 or $30 should really put anything you need to just get an enterprise level and price it at $250 or $500. This is free money.
Competitors are a wonderful thing because it's an engraved invitation from God that tells you that there's money to be made in a particular place.

People who are spending essentially other people's money, it just comes out of a budget so it doesn't matter to them whether it's 200 or 250 or 500. As long as it still only requires one signature or zero signatures it's whatever it is.
Our business grew even when I was on practically bed rest, and that was a really transformative moment for me.

People will happily pay you money, even if you're not working on the product every day, because people don't care if you're working on the product every day, they only care what they're getting out of it.

If you compare yourself to other people and think you're not successful unless you're beating them by some metric you will generally be less happy than you are if you're comparing yourself against either where you were previously or what your goals are.
So it's cargo-culting mixed with social contagion.
key_insight: Product businesses selling to other businesses on value — not cost — can generate life-changing revenue on remarkably few hours of work, but founders must overcome both cultural pressure to overwork and the instinct to underprice.
